close_reason: PRD reviewed and approved. Killer features integrated (4 P0 + 2 P1), new personas added (Product Owner, Domain Expert), timeline updated to 7-phase 4+1 strategy, all sections consistent.

title: Create Architecture doc informed by DDD and spikes
description:
## Goal
Create docs/ARCHITECTURE.md using ARCHITECTURE_TEMPLATE.md, informed by DDD artifacts, spike results, and all 7 killer features.

## Research Input: k7m.5 DDD Artifacts
docs/DDD.md is now complete and provides the primary input for architecture:
- **9 bounded contexts**: Guided Discovery, Domain Model, Architecture Testing, Ticket Pipeline, Ticket Freshness, Tool Translation, Knowledge Base, Bootstrap (orchestration), Rescue
- **Context map**: integration patterns (domain events, queries, ACLs, orchestration)
- **10 subdomains**: 4 Core (hexagonal), 3 Supporting (layered), 3 Generic (ACL wrapper)
- **Complexity budget YAML**: treatment levels for architecture, testing, fitness functions, tickets
- **5 Core aggregates**: DiscoverySession, DomainModel, FitnessTestSuite, TicketPlan, RippleReview — each with invariants
- **4 open questions** in DDD Section 8 that architecture must address

## Background
Per project lifecycle: README → PRD → DDD → **Architecture** → Implementation.
Architecture must be informed by:
- Bounded contexts from docs/DDD.md
- CLI + MCP design from spike alty-k7m.4
- Knowledge base structure from spike alty-k7m.1
- Multi-tool config formats from spike alty-k7m.3
- Fitness function generation design from spike alty-k7m.10
- Ticket pipeline design from spike alty-k7m.11
- Ripple review design from spike alty-k7m.12

### Research Input: k7m.1

Spike k7m.1 (Knowledge base structure for tool conventions) produced the following outputs that directly inform architecture:
- **TOML-based `.alty/knowledge/` directory structure**: tool conventions stored as structured TOML files organized by category/tool/topic
- **KnowledgeLookupPort O(1) path resolution**: lookups via `(category, tool, topic, version)` triple map directly to filesystem paths, enabling O(1) resolution without indexing
- **Drift detection schema**: each TOML entry includes a `[_meta]` section with `source_hash`, `last_verified`, and `schema_version` fields for staleness detection
- **Cross-tool generation matrix**: `cross-tool/generation-matrix.toml` defines which knowledge entries map to which tool config outputs, enabling multi-tool config generation from a single knowledge source

### Research Input: k7m.10, k7m.11, k7m.12 Spike Results

- **Shared YAML IR** (`.alty/domain-model.yaml`): Cross-cutting artifact consumed by fitness function generation (k7m.10) and ticket pipeline (k7m.11). Must be documented as a first-class architectural component with a Pydantic schema. Both generators read this file as input — it is the machine-readable representation of DDD.md.
- **Fitness functions (k7m.10)**: Hybrid import-linter TOML + pytestarch .py approach. FitnessTestSuite aggregate validates 5 invariants before emitting files. Contract strictness driven by complexity budget: Core=4 types, Supporting=2, Generic=1. Generation pipeline: YAML IR → FitnessTestSuite domain model → import-linter contracts + pytestarch tests. See `docs/research/20260223_fitness_function_design.md`
- **Ticket pipeline (k7m.11)**: Kahn's algorithm topological sort for dependency ordering. 3-tier detail levels (FULL/STANDARD/STUB) driven by complexity budget. BeadsWriterPort must call `bd create` + `bd dep add` with rollback on failure. TicketPlan aggregate validates 5 invariants. See `docs/research/20260223_ticket_pipeline_design.md`
- **Ripple review (k7m.12)**: Uses beads labels+comments (no schema changes needed). 4-step after-close protocol designed for generated CLAUDE.md. Two-tier stub/full ticket promotion rules. `alty ticket-health` command for read-only health report. bd-ripple bug found: allows empty context diffs (violates invariant #1). See `docs/research/20260223_ripple_review_design.md`

## Architecture Must Cover All 7 Killer Features

| # | Feature | Architecture Concern |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Architecture Fitness Functions | Generation pipeline: shared YAML IR → FitnessTestSuite aggregate → hybrid import-linter TOML + pytestarch .py files |
| 2 | Domain Story → Ticket Pipeline | Generation pipeline: shared YAML IR → TicketPlan aggregate (Kahn's sort) → bd create + bd dep add with rollback |
| 3 | Complexity Budget | Classification engine: subdomain → Core/Supporting/Generic → treatment levels (drives fitness contract strictness + ticket detail level) |
| 4 | Tool-Native Context Translation | Config generation: domain model → .claude/, .cursor/, etc. via cross-tool/generation-matrix.toml |
| 5 | Rescue Mode | Analysis engine: existing project → gap report → migration tickets |
| 6 | Living Knowledge Base | RLM-addressable store: tool conventions, version tracking, drift detection |
| 7 | Ticket Freshness & Ripple Review | Event-driven flow: close → bd-ripple (labels+comments) → 4-step after-close protocol → alty ticket-health report |

## 5 Personas
Architecture must support both technical (Solo Dev, Team Lead, Tool Switcher) and non-technical users (Product Owner, Domain Expert). This affects CLI UX layer, question flow adaptation, and output formatting.

## Research Input: k7m.4 CLI/MCP Design
docs/research/20260222_cli_mcp_design.md provides the interface architecture:
- **CLI framework**: Typer 0.24.1 (ADR accepted) — type hints = CLI interface
- **MCP SDK**: official `mcp` v1.26.0 with FastMCP — `@mcp.tool()` + type annotations = auto-schema
- **15 CLI commands** mapped 1:1 to bounded context entry points
- **13 application layer ports** (Protocols) shared between CLI and MCP adapters
- **Composition root** at `src/infrastructure/composition.py` — wires all ports at startup
- **Dependency flow**: CLI/MCP → Application Ports → Domain Models → Infrastructure Adapters
- **Entry points**: `vs` (Typer CLI) and `vs-mcp` (FastMCP stdio server) in pyproject.toml
- **4 DDD open questions answered** in design report § 7 (MCP is adapter not BC, doc-health separate from ticket freshness, KB curated-only in P0, rescue asks user for classification)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] docs/ARCHITECTURE.md created from template
- [ ] Layer architecture maps to DDD bounded contexts
- [ ] CLI (vs) and MCP server components defined
- [ ] .alty/ directory structure finalized
- [ ] Shared application core (ports pattern) between CLI and MCP documented
- [ ] Knowledge base RLM lookup paths documented, referencing the URI scheme `alty://knowledge/tools/{tool}/{subtopic}?version={version}` from k7m.1
- [ ] **Shared YAML IR schema** (`.alty/domain-model.yaml`) documented as cross-cutting architectural component consumed by fitness functions and ticket pipeline
- [ ] **Fitness function generation pipeline documented** — hybrid import-linter TOML + pytestarch, driven by FitnessTestSuite aggregate and shared YAML IR
- [ ] **Ticket pipeline architecture documented** — Kahn's algorithm topological sort, 3-tier detail levels (FULL/STANDARD/STUB), formal `bd dep add` via BeadsWriterPort with rollback
- [ ] **Complexity budget engine documented** — classification → enforcement points (fitness contract strictness + ticket detail level)
- [ ] **Multi-tool config generation documented**, referencing `cross-tool/generation-matrix.toml` from k7m.1 as the mapping source
- [ ] **Ripple review flow documented** — beads labels+comments (no schema changes), 4-step after-close protocol for generated CLAUDE.md, two-tier stub/full promotion
- [ ] **Rescue mode analysis pipeline documented** (scan → gap report → migration plan)
- [ ] Security section covers file safety rules
- [ ] All design decisions traceable to PRD or spike ADRs
close_reason: All 15 AC items satisfied. ARCHITECTURE.md is 1235 lines covering all 21 P0 PRD capabilities, 9 bounded contexts, 7 killer feature architectures, 12 ADRs, and full CLI/MCP interface design.

title: Spike: Gap analysis design for existing projects
description:
## Research Question

How should alty init --existing analyze an existing project and produce a useful gap report? What should it scan, what gaps matter, and how should the report be structured?

## Timebox
3 hours

## Background
- Applying alty to an existing project is complex — existing files, conventions, partial structure
- Must create a new git branch (alty/init) for all changes
- Must produce a gap report: what's missing, what conflicts, what's already compliant
- Must ask clarifying domain questions to adapt agent profiles and DDD artifacts
- **HARD GATE: existing tests must pass after scaffolding. If any test fails, roll back everything.**
- **Two-phase approach (from PRD):**
  - **P0: Basic scaffold overlay** — detect existing structure, generate .alty/ and tool configs without breaking anything, branch-based with preview
  - **P1: Structural migration** — scan for implicit bounded contexts, identify anemic models, generate migration tickets with before/after test verification
  - This spike should design both phases but clearly separate them
- **5 Personas:** A Product Owner running `--existing` on a team's repo has different needs than a developer. PO may want a high-level health report ("your project is missing X, Y, Z"). Developer wants detailed gap analysis with file paths. Domain Expert may be asked to validate the detected domain language.

## Investigation Approach
1. Define what a 'fully-seeded' project looks like (the golden state)
2. Design scan targets: docs, tooling, structure, tests, quality gates
3. Design gap categories: missing, conflicting, partial, compliant
4. Design gap report format (terminal output + optional md file)
5. Design the branch workflow (create, populate, leave for user to review)
6. **Design test verification step**: detect test runner (pytest, unittest, etc.), run existing suite, compare before/after, rollback on failure
7. Consider: how to detect existing domain language from code/docs
8. **Separate P0 (scaffold overlay) from P1 (structural migration) clearly** — what's in each phase?
9. **Design persona-adapted output** — how does the gap report differ for PO vs developer?

## Expected Deliverables
- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_gap_analysis_design.md
- [ ] Gap categories and scan targets list
- [ ] Gap report format (terminal + markdown)
- [ ] Branch workflow design with rollback mechanism
- [ ] **Test verification design** (detect runner, run, compare, rollback)
- [ ] **P0 vs P1 scope separation** (what goes in basic overlay vs structural migration)
- [ ] **Persona-adapted output notes** (PO summary vs developer detail)
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for implementation
close_reason: Research report complete (1224 lines, 13 sections). Covers golden state definition, 5 gap categories, scan targets, branch workflow with rollback pseudocode, test verification with baseline comparison, P0/P1 scope separation, persona-adapted output, domain language detection (P1), GapAnalysis aggregate domain model, competitive analysis (6 tools), and 17 follow-up implementation tickets.

title: Spike: Competitive landscape & killer features prioritization
description:
## Research Question

Which killer features should alty prioritize to differentiate from MetaGPT, Pythagora, Lovable/Bolt/v0, GPT-Engineer, and PRD generators (WriteMyPrd, Tara AI)?

## Timebox
3 hours

## Background

Market research (stored in .notes/killer-features-analysis.md) identified 6 potential killer features:

1. **Architecture Fitness Functions** — Generate executable boundary tests from bounded context maps. Architecture enforces itself via test suite, survives contact with any AI coding tool.
2. **Domain Story → Ticket Pipeline** — Auto-generate dependency-ordered beads tickets from DDD artifacts. Each ticket pre-filled with TDD phases, SOLID mapping, and acceptance criteria from domain invariants.
3. **Rescue Mode / Structural Migration** — alty init --existing goes beyond scaffolding overlay. Scans for implicit bounded contexts, identifies anemic models, generates migration tickets with before/after test verification.
4. **Tool-Native Context Translation** — One domain model generates native config for Claude Code (CLAUDE.md + agents), Cursor (.cursorrules + .cursor/mcp.json), Antigravity, OpenCode. Configs contain domain-specific language and boundary rules, not generic boilerplate.
5. **Complexity Budget** — Classify subdomains during DDD discovery (Core/Supporting/Generic). Enforce investment level: full DDD for core, simple services for supporting, CRUD/off-the-shelf for generic. Fitness functions flag over-engineering in generic domains.
6. **Living Knowledge Base with Drift Detection** — .alty/knowledge/ tracks tool versions, detects convention changes, flags stale architecture docs vs actual code structure.

## Competitive Landscape Summary

| Category | Competitors | Their Gap |
|----------|------------|-----------|
| AI Software Company | MetaGPT, Pythagora | Autonomous/complex, no boundary enforcement after generation |
| Rapid Vibe Coding | Lovable, Bolt, v0, GPT-Engineer | Skip architecture entirely, fail at scale |
| Execution Tools | Cursor, Claude Code, Copilot | Partners not competitors — need structured context we provide |
| PRD Generators | WriteMyPrd, Tara AI, IBM ERM | No software architecture understanding |

**White space: "Architectural Discipline as a Service"**

## Investigation Steps

1. **Validate features against user personas**
   - Solo developer starting a new project — which features matter most?
   - Team lead adopting alty on existing codebase — which features matter most?
   - Developer switching between AI tools — which features matter most?

2. **Classify each feature by phase**
   - P0 (launch): Must have for initial release
   - P1 (growth): Drives adoption after launch
   - P2 (moat): Long-term competitive advantage

3. **Assess build complexity**
   - Which features can be built incrementally?
   - Which require foundational work in Phase 1?
   - Which have dependencies on other spikes (k7m.1, k7m.2, k7m.3, k7m.4, k7m.8)?

4. **Map features to PRD sections**
   - Which existing PRD capabilities cover these features?
   - Which need new sections added to the PRD?
   - Which change the priority of existing capabilities?

5. **Identify feature interactions**
   - Fitness functions + complexity budget = budget-aware boundary tests
   - DDD questions + ticket pipeline = end-to-end automation
   - Tool translation + knowledge base = version-aware config generation

## Expected Deliverables

- [ ] Research report at docs/research/YYYYMMDD_killer_features.md
- [ ] Feature prioritization matrix (impact vs effort vs moat depth per feature)
- [ ] Phase assignment for each feature (P0/P1/P2)
- [ ] Feature-to-PRD mapping (which PRD sections need updates)
- [ ] Feature dependency map (which features depend on which spikes)
- [ ] Recommended PRD changes for k7m.6 review
- [ ] Follow-up tickets for Phase 2 epic
close_reason: All 7 deliverables complete: research report with prioritization matrix, phase assignments, PRD mapping, dependency map, PRD change recommendations, and follow-up tickets (k7m.10, k7m.11) created.

title: Bug: bd-ripple flags empty ticket ID, label doesn't persist
description:
## Goal / Problem

`bin/bd-ripple` silently fails in multiple ways after closing a ticket: it reports flagging a ticket but the ticket ID is blank, the label doesn't persist, and comments fail. This undermines the after-close protocol's reliability.

**Observed behavior:**
- Script output: `✓  — flagged with review_needed` (blank ID before the dash)
- `bd query label=review_needed` returns nothing after script runs
- Manual `bd label add alty-k7m.28 review_needed` works fine

**Trigger:** Closing alty-k7m.8 and running `bin/bd-ripple alty-k7m.8`

## Background / Context

- bd-ripple is the event-driven ticket freshness flagging script (177 lines bash)
- It traverses parent-child siblings, dependents (blocks), and related links
- The after-close protocol depends on it for Step 1 (ripple review)
- When it fails silently, flagged tickets get missed and the review step is skipped

## Root Causes

### RC1: Empty string in TO_FLAG array from heredoc parsing
Lines 95-97, 110-112, 124-126: When the Python script produces no output, the `while IFS= read -r` loop still executes once with an empty string. The `[[ -n "$sib" ]]` guard should catch this, but the empty string still enters the array in some cases (possibly trailing newline).

### RC2: Wrong label command
Line 157 uses `bd update "$tid" --add-label review_needed` but the working command throughout the codebase is `bd label add <id> review_needed`. The `--add-label` flag on `bd update` may not exist or may silently fail.

### RC3: No traversable relationships for orphan tickets
k7m.8 has no formal parent-child relationship (not a child of the k7m epic via `bd dep add`) and no blocks/blocked-by dependents in its JSON. The script can't traverse to k7m.28 (which should be flagged) because there's no graph edge connecting them. This is a data issue (missing formal deps) but the script should warn when it finds zero traversable relationships for a ticket that clearly belongs to an epic.

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Ticket Freshness |
| Ubiquitous Language | ripple review, review_needed, context diff |
| Layer | Infrastructure (bash script, beads CLI adapter) |

## Steps

1. Fix RC2: Replace `bd update "$tid" --add-label review_needed` with `bd label add "$tid" review_needed` on line 157
2. Fix RC1: Guard against empty strings in TO_FLAG array — filter empty elements after dedup (line 129) or use `mapfile` with process substitution instead of heredoc piping
3. Fix RC3: Add a warning when the script finds zero tickets to flag for a ticket that has a known epic parent (check `bd show` for epic membership even without formal parent-child dep)
4. Add integration test: close a ticket, run bd-ripple, verify label persists and comment exists

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `bd label add` used instead of `bd update --add-label` for flagging
- [ ] Empty strings never enter TO_FLAG array (tested with ticket that has no deps)
- [ ] Script warns when zero traversable relationships found
- [ ] Label persists after script runs (verified with `bd query label=review_needed`)
- [ ] Comment is successfully added to flagged ticket
- [ ] Manual test: close a ticket, run bd-ripple, verify output shows correct ticket IDs

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| No deps at all | Ticket with no parent, no dependents, no related | Warning: no traversable relationships |
| All siblings closed | Only closed siblings exist | No tickets to flag (clean exit) |
| Empty string in array | Python outputs nothing | TO_FLAG array stays empty |
| Label already exists | Ticket already has review_needed | Skip label add, still add comment |

## Quality Gates

```bash
# Since this is a bash script, quality gates are manual testing
shellcheck bin/bd-ripple          # Static analysis
# Manual: close a test ticket, run bd-ripple, verify behavior
```

- [ ] shellcheck passes with no errors
- [ ] Manual test passes end-to-end

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on beads CLI (`bd`) label and comment commands working correctly
- The --add-label flag behavior should be verified against beads docs
close_reason: Fixed empty-array dedup bug in bd-ripple: guarded readarray with empty check, added grep -v to filter empty strings, added helpful hint when zero traversable relationships found. Shellcheck passes clean.

title: Epic 1: Go Migration — Domain Layer
description:
## Goal / Problem

Migrate the entire Python domain layer (~10,000 LoC, 14 bounded contexts) to idiomatic Go using Claude Code teammates in parallel. This is the foundation layer — application and infrastructure layers depend on it.

## Ticket Structure

```
ok6.1 (scaffold) ✓
ok6.2 (agent templates) ✓
ok6.3 (team prompt) ✓
ok6.11 (domain services — absorbed into ok6.7/8/9) ✓
ok6.4 (shared kernel) ← unblocked, dev-core
  ├→ ok6.6 (DDD artifacts: DomainModel + values) — dev-core
  ├→ ok6.7 (Discovery + Bootstrap + services) — dev-discovery ──┐
  ├→ ok6.8 (Ticket + Freshness + Implementability + services) — dev-ticket ──┤
  └→ ok6.9 (8 feature contexts + services) — dev-features ──┤
                                                              └→ ok6.12 (cross-context integration tests) — qa-engineer
```

**Deferred to Epic 2:** ok6.5 (Watermill event bus), ok6.10 (port interfaces)

## DDD Alignment

| Bounded Context | Impact |
|----------------|--------|
| Shared kernel (errors, VOs, events) | Go sentinel errors + shared value objects |
| DDD Artifacts (DomainModel, UbiquitousLanguage) | Go structs with unexported fields |
| Discovery + Bootstrap | Translate aggregates + state machines |
| Ticket Pipeline + Freshness + Implementability | Translate plan + renderer + validator |
| Fitness + Quality + Knowledge + Rescue + Research + DocHealth + Challenge + ToolTranslation | Translate 8 feature contexts |

## Key Decisions

- `quality_gate.py` moved to shared kernel (dev-core) — imported by stack_profile and ticket_detail_renderer across contexts
- Domain services absorbed into their owning context tickets (no separate ok6.11)
- Agent templates updated inline in existing `.claude/agents/` files (no separate `-go.md` files)

## Success Metrics

- All Python domain tests have Go equivalents (test parity >= 95%)
- `go build ./internal/...` succeeds with zero errors
- `go test ./internal/... -v -race` passes with >= 80% coverage
- `golangci-lint run` passes
- Zero cross-layer imports (verified by `internal/` + go-arch-lint)

## Scope

**In scope:**
- All files under `src/domain/` (models, services, events, errors)
- All tests under `tests/domain/`
- Domain error types (sentinel errors in Go)
- Domain event types (Go structs)
- Domain service functions
- Cross-context integration tests

**Out of scope (Epic 2):**
- Application layer (ports, handlers)
- Watermill event bus wiring
- Infrastructure layer (adapters, CLI, MCP)
- CI/CD pipeline setup

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ok6.4 closed: shared kernel translated with test parity
- [ ] ok6.6 closed: DomainModel + UbiquitousLanguage translated
- [ ] ok6.7 closed: Discovery + Bootstrap contexts translated
- [ ] ok6.8 closed: Ticket + Freshness + Implementability translated
- [ ] ok6.9 closed: 8 feature contexts translated
- [ ] ok6.12 closed: cross-context integration tests pass, parity >= 95%
- [ ] `go build ./internal/...` passes
- [ ] `go test ./internal/... -v -race` passes
- [ ] `golangci-lint run` passes
- [ ] `go-arch-lint check` passes

## Reference

- docs/research/20260306_5_go_rewrite_evaluation.md
- docs/research/20260307_3_go_migration_team_execution.md
- docs/research/20260307_2_go_team_development_patterns.md

title: Go project scaffold: go.mod, Makefile, .golangci.yml, internal/ structure
description:
## Goal / Problem

Create the Go project scaffold — directory structure, build system, linter configs, and one reference domain type with test. This is the foundation all migration agents build on.

## Background / Context

- Research: docs/research/20260306_5_go_rewrite_evaluation.md (Go Architecture Design section)
- Research: docs/research/20260307_2_go_team_development_patterns.md (quality gates, Makefile, .golangci.yml)
- Research: docs/research/20260307_4_go_project_setup_best_practices.md (module path, DDD structure, linter configs)
- Research: docs/research/20260307_6_watermill_cqrs_deep_dive.md (event infrastructure patterns)
- Pattern reference: ThreeDotsLabs Wild Workouts (github.com/ThreeDotsLabs/wild-workouts-go-ddd-example)

### Resolved Decisions

**Project location:** golang branch in existing repo. Python source stays as living reference during migration; delete Python files as Go equivalents are verified. See 20260307_4 §1.

**Go module path:** github.com/alty-cli/alty — follows Go convention (hosting-platform/org/project). See 20260307_4 §2.

**DDD structure:** Bounded-context-first under internal/ (Wild Workouts pattern):
  internal/{context}/domain/, internal/{context}/application/, internal/{context}/infrastructure/
  with internal/shared/ for cross-cutting concerns. See 20260307_4 §3.

**Linter strategy:** Three-layer enforcement:
  1. internal/ package — compiler prevents external imports
  2. depguard (golangci-lint) — prevents domain→application/infrastructure imports
  3. go-arch-lint — component-level mayDependOn rules
  See 20260307_4 §6.

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Shared infrastructure (project scaffold) |
| Ubiquitous Language | InvariantViolationError, BoundedContext, DomainModel |
| Layer | All layers (directory structure) |

## Design

### Directory Structure

cmd/alty/main.go, cmd/alty-mcp/main.go (entry points)
internal/{bootstrap,discovery,ticket,fitness,quality,knowledge,rescue,research}/domain/
internal/{bootstrap,discovery,ticket,fitness,quality,knowledge,rescue,research}/application/
internal/{bootstrap,discovery,ticket,fitness,quality,knowledge,rescue,research}/infrastructure/
internal/shared/domain/errors/, internal/shared/domain/ddd/, internal/shared/domain/events/
internal/shared/domain/valueobjects/

### Data Models

| Model | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| BoundedContext | Value Object | Reference type proving the scaffold works |
| InvariantViolationError | Sentinel Error | Domain error pattern |
| ErrNotFound | Sentinel Error | Standard not-found |
| ErrAlreadyExists | Sentinel Error | Idempotency guard |

### Dependencies

| Package | Purpose | Version |
|---------|---------|---------|
| github.com/spf13/cobra | CLI framework | latest |
| github.com/stretchr/testify | Test assertions | latest |

## SOLID Mapping

| Principle | Implementation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **S**ingle Responsibility | Each bounded context directory owns its domain |
| **O**pen/Closed | internal/ structure allows adding contexts without modifying existing |
| **L**iskov Substitution | N/A (scaffold only) |
| **I**nterface Segregation | N/A (scaffold only) |
| **D**ependency Inversion | depguard + go-arch-lint enforce domain → ports only |

## TDD Workflow

### RED Phase

Write test: internal/shared/domain/ddd/bounded_context_test.go

```
TestNewBoundedContext_ValidInput: Creates valid BoundedContext
TestNewBoundedContext_EmptyName: Returns ErrInvariantViolation
TestNewBoundedContext_WithAggregates: Stores aggregate list
TestNewBoundedContext_NilAggregates: Valid with empty list
TestNewBoundedContext_DuplicateAggregates: Returns error
```

Run: go test ./internal/shared/domain/ddd/... → should FAIL

### GREEN Phase

Create internal/shared/domain/ddd/bounded_context.go:
- Unexported struct fields (name, aggregates)
- NewBoundedContext() constructor with validation
- Exported getter methods

Run: go test ./internal/shared/domain/ddd/... → should PASS

### REFACTOR Phase

- Verify gofumpt formatting
- Verify golangci-lint passes
- Verify go-arch-lint passes

## Steps

1. Initialize go.mod in the golang branch with module path github.com/alty-cli/alty
2. Create full internal/ directory tree — bounded-context-first structure with .gitkeep where empty
3. Create cmd/alty/main.go with minimal Cobra root command
4. Create cmd/alty-mcp/main.go with placeholder
5. Create Makefile with targets: build, test, lint, vet, fmt, check, release
6. Create .golangci.yml v2 config from 20260307_4 research (depguard DDD rules, gofumpt formatter)
7. Create .go-arch-lint.yml with component mayDependOn rules from 20260307_4 research
8. Create internal/shared/domain/errors/errors.go with sentinel errors
9. Write test: internal/shared/domain/ddd/bounded_context_test.go (table-driven, 5+ cases)
10. Write impl: internal/shared/domain/ddd/bounded_context.go (unexported fields, NewBoundedContext constructor)
11. Run make check — verify all gates pass

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| Empty context name | NewBoundedContext("", nil) | ErrInvariantViolation |
| Nil aggregates | NewBoundedContext("Orders", nil) | Valid (empty aggregates list) |
| Duplicate aggregates | NewBoundedContext("Orders", ["Order", "Order"]) | ErrInvariantViolation |
| Very long name | NewBoundedContext(string of 1000 chars, nil) | Valid (no max length for now) |

## Quality Gates

go build ./...
go test ./... -v -race
go vet ./...
golangci-lint run
gofumpt -l .

- [ ] All gates pass
- [ ] Reference type has >= 80% coverage

## Pre-Implementation Validation

- [x] Go module path decided: github.com/alty-cli/alty (20260307_4)
- [x] Project location decided: golang branch in alty-cli repo (keep Python as reference)
- [ ] golangci-lint v2 installable (go install github.com/golangci/golangci-lint/v2/cmd/golangci-lint@latest)
- [ ] go-arch-lint installable (go install github.com/fe3dback/go-arch-lint@latest)
- [ ] Cobra installable (go get github.com/spf13/cobra@latest)

## QA Before Close

- [ ] go build succeeds on empty project
- [ ] go test passes with reference type
- [ ] golangci-lint passes
- [ ] go-arch-lint passes
- [ ] Directory structure matches bounded-context-first design
- [ ] Reference type demonstrates pattern (unexported fields, constructor, table-driven test)

## Risks / Dependencies

- golangci-lint v2 config syntax (differs from v1 — use migrate command if needed)
- go-arch-lint YAML syntax may need iteration

title: Port interfaces: 25 Go interfaces from 22 application/ports files
description:
## Goal / Problem

Translate 25 Python Protocol classes (across 22 port files in `src/application/ports/`) to Go interfaces. These interfaces define the contracts between application handlers and infrastructure adapters. All downstream tickets (4xd.2–4xd.5) depend on these interfaces existing first.

## Background / Context

- **Research:** `docs/research/20260306_5_go_rewrite_evaluation.md` section 3 — "Application ports: Trivial" (500 LoC, Protocol → Go interface)
- **Research:** `docs/research/20260307_4_go_project_setup_best_practices.md` — Go convention: define interfaces at point of use
- **Pattern reference:** Python Protocols in `src/application/ports/`
- **Epic 1 domain layer** is complete — domain types referenced by ports already exist in Go

### Port Inventory (22 files → 25 Protocol classes)

| Port File | Protocol(s) | Go Target Package |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| `artifact_generation_port.py` | `ArtifactRendererPort` | `internal/discovery/application/` |
| `beads_writer_port.py` | `BeadsWriterPort` | `internal/ticket/application/` |
| `bootstrap_port.py` | `BootstrapPort` | `internal/bootstrap/application/` |
| `challenger_port.py` | `ChallengerPort` | `internal/challenge/application/` |
| `config_generation_port.py` | `ConfigGenerationPort` | `internal/tooltranslation/application/` |
| `discovery_port.py` | `DiscoveryPort` | `internal/discovery/application/` |
| `doc_health_port.py` | `DocHealthPort` | `internal/dochealth/application/` |
| `doc_review_port.py` | `DocReviewPort` | `internal/dochealth/application/` |
| `domain_research_port.py` | `DomainResearchPort` | `internal/research/application/` |
| `drift_detection_port.py` | `DriftDetectionPort` | `internal/knowledge/application/` |
| `file_writer_port.py` | `FileWriterPort` | `internal/shared/application/` |
| `fitness_generation_port.py` | `FitnessGenerationPort` | `internal/fitness/application/` |
| `knowledge_lookup_port.py` | `KnowledgeLookupPort` | `internal/knowledge/application/` |
| `persona_port.py` | `PersonaPort` | `internal/tooltranslation/application/` |
| `quality_gate_port.py` | `GateRunnerProtocol`, `QualityGatePort` (2) | `internal/fitness/application/` |
| `rescue_port.py` | `ProjectScanPort`, `GitOpsPort`, `RescuePort` (3) | `internal/rescue/application/` |
| `spike_follow_up_port.py` | `SpikeFollowUpPort` | `internal/research/application/` |
| `spike_report_parser_port.py` | `SpikeReportParserPort` | `internal/research/application/` |
| `ticket_generation_port.py` | `TicketGenerationPort` | `internal/ticket/application/` |
| `ticket_health_port.py` | `TicketHealthPort` | `internal/ticket/application/` |
| `tool_detection_port.py` | `ToolDetectionPort` | `internal/discovery/application/` |
| `web_search_port.py` | `WebSearchPort` | `internal/research/application/` |

### Go Convention: Interface at Point of Use

Go idiom is to define interfaces where they are consumed (in the handler's package), not in a separate `ports/` package. Each interface goes into the bounded context's `application/` package alongside its handler.

### Translation Pattern

```python
# Python
class BeadsWriterPort(Protocol):
    def create_epic(self, title: str, description: str, priority: int = 1) -> str: ...
```

```go
// Go — internal/ticket/application/ports.go
type BeadsWriter interface {
    CreateEpic(title, description string, priority int) (string, error)
}
```

Key differences:
- `Protocol` → Go `interface` (implicit satisfaction, no explicit registration)
- Method names: `snake_case` → `PascalCase`
- Return types: add `error` return for fallible operations
- Optional params: use functional options or separate methods
- No `self` parameter

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | All contexts (ports distributed across 10 bounded contexts) |
| Ubiquitous Language | Port, Interface, Writer, Reader, Scanner, Generator, Runner |
| Layer | Application (contracts between application and infrastructure) |

## SOLID Mapping

| Principle | Implementation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **S** | Each interface has one focused responsibility |
| **O** | New adapters implement existing interfaces without changing them |
| **L** | All implementations must honor interface contracts |
| **I** | ISP enforced — `rescue_port.py` splits into 3 separate interfaces |
| **D** | Handlers depend on these interfaces, never on concrete adapters |

## TDD Workflow

### RED Phase

Write compile-time satisfaction tests for each interface:
```go
var _ BeadsWriter = (*MockBeadsWriter)(nil)
```

Write interface contract tests that mock implementations must satisfy.

### GREEN Phase

1. Read each Python Protocol class
2. Create Go interface in the bounded context's `application/` package
3. Group related interfaces in a single `ports.go` file per context
4. Ensure method signatures match Python Protocol (with Go conventions)

### REFACTOR Phase

- Verify no interface has more than 5 methods (ISP)
- Verify interface names match ubiquitous language
- Verify `go vet ./...` passes

## Steps

1. For each of 10 bounded contexts, create `internal/{context}/application/ports.go`
2. Translate Protocol classes to Go interfaces (25 total)
3. Create mock implementations for testing (one per interface)
4. Write compile-time satisfaction checks
5. Special: `rescue_port.py` has 3 Protocols in one file — split into 3 Go interfaces
6. Special: `quality_gate_port.py` has 2 Protocols — keep both in same file

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 25 Go interfaces defined across 10 bounded context `application/` packages
- [ ] Method signatures match Python Protocol signatures (with Go conventions)
- [ ] ISP enforced — no interface has more than 5 methods
- [ ] Compile-time satisfaction checks for each interface
- [ ] Mock implementations created for downstream handler testing
- [ ] `go build ./...` passes
- [ ] `go vet ./...` passes

## Quality Gates

```bash
go build ./...
go vet ./...
golangci-lint run
```

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on: 4xd.1 (ARCHITECTURE.md must document Go interface conventions first)
- Depends on: ok6.4, ok6.6-9 (domain types referenced by ports — all CLOSED ✓)
- Risk: Some Python Protocols use `async def` — Go interfaces should use sync signatures (goroutines are a caller concern)

title: Cross-context integration tests + event flow validation
description:
## Goal
Write cross-context integration tests that verify interactions between bounded contexts translated in ok6.6–ok6.9. These tests validate that shared kernel types work correctly across context boundaries and that event contracts are honored.

## DDD Alignment
- **Scope**: Cross-cutting integration tests spanning multiple bounded contexts
- **Go packages**: `internal/integration/` or context-level `_test.go` files
- **Depends on**: ok6.6 (DDD artifacts), ok6.7 (Discovery+Bootstrap), ok6.8 (Ticket), ok6.9 (Features)

## Test Categories

### 1. Shared Kernel Interop
- Value objects from `internal/shared/domain/` used correctly by all contexts
- SubdomainClassification, Tier, StackProfile consumed without import errors
- Event types from shared events package used by context-specific event producers

### 2. Event Contract Verification
- Discovery events consumed by downstream contexts (Bootstrap, Ticket)
- Ticket events reference valid shared types
- Fitness events reference valid domain model types
- All event structs are serializable (round-trip marshal/unmarshal)

### 3. Cross-Context Type Compatibility
- DomainModel aggregate (ok6.6) works with Ticket plan generation (ok6.8)
- BoundedContext classification (shared) drives tier classification (ticket context)
- Stack detection results (discovery) feed into tool adapter selection (tooltranslation)

### 4. Python Parity Check
- Count Go tests vs Python tests per context
- Verify no Python test assertions were dropped during translation
- Report coverage delta

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Cross-context integration test suite compiles: `go build ./...`
- [ ] All integration tests pass: `go test ./... -v -race`
- [ ] Event contract tests verify serialization round-trips
- [ ] Shared kernel types work across all 10 bounded context packages
- [ ] Python test parity report: Go test count >= Python test count per context
- [ ] No import cycle violations: `go vet ./...`
- [ ] Full domain layer lint clean: `golangci-lint run`

## Owner
qa-engineer

title: Deploy Go-specific agent templates to .claude/agents/
description:
## Goal / Problem

Create Go-optimized agent templates for the migration team. The existing Python-focused agents (developer.md, tech-lead.md, qa-engineer.md, project-manager.md) reference ruff/mypy/pytest and Python conventions. Go migration needs agents that reference go build/test/vet/golangci-lint and Go DDD conventions.

## Background / Context

- Agent designs: docs/research/20260307_3_go_migration_team_execution.md (Q2 section — full template text)
- Go patterns: docs/research/20260307_2_go_team_development_patterns.md
- Existing agents: .claude/agents/developer.md, tech-lead.md, qa-engineer.md, project-manager.md

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Tool Translation (config generation for AI coding tools) |
| Ubiquitous Language | agent, persona, quality gate, translation protocol |
| Layer | Infrastructure (agent config files, not domain logic) |

## Design

### Files to Create

| File | Based On | Key Changes |
|------|----------|-------------|
| .claude/agents/developer-go.md | developer.md + research report | Go quality gates, table-driven tests, translation protocol |
| .claude/agents/tech-lead-go.md | tech-lead.md + research report | Go layer violation check, interface satisfaction, error review |
| .claude/agents/qa-engineer-go.md | qa-engineer.md + research report | Python test parity verification, race detector, Go coverage |
| .claude/agents/project-manager-go.md | project-manager.md | Go quality gates, Go project lifecycle |

These are SEPARATE files (not replacing Python agents). Both sets coexist.

### No Code — Documentation Only

These are markdown agent persona files. No TDD applies. Quality is verified by reviewing content against research report templates and ensuring no Python-specific commands remain.

## SOLID Mapping

N/A — these are documentation files, not code.

## TDD Workflow

N/A — no production code. Validation is content review against research report templates.

## Steps

1. Read research report Q2 section for full agent template text
2. Create .claude/agents/developer-go.md — copy from research report, verify Go conventions
3. Create .claude/agents/tech-lead-go.md — copy from research report, verify review checklist
4. Create .claude/agents/qa-engineer-go.md — copy from research report, verify test commands
5. Create .claude/agents/project-manager-go.md — adapt existing PM, replace Python refs with Go
6. Verify: grep for "python\|pytest\|ruff\|mypy\|uv run" in all 4 files — must find ZERO matches
7. Verify: all files reference go build/test/vet/golangci-lint

## Edge Cases

N/A — documentation files.

## Quality Gates

No code quality gates. Instead:

- [ ] grep -i "python\|pytest\|ruff\|mypy\|uv run\|pip" .claude/agents/*-go.md returns zero matches
- [ ] Each file has: frontmatter (name, description, tools, model), key documents, primary responsibilities, quality commands
- [ ] Anti-hallucination rules present in developer-go.md and tech-lead-go.md
- [ ] Translation protocol (read Python → write Go test → write Go code) present in developer-go.md

## Pre-Implementation Validation

- [ ] Research report Q2 section has complete template text for all 4 agents
- [ ] Existing Python agent files are available as structural reference
- [ ] ok6.1 scaffold exists (to verify quality gate commands reference correct paths)

## QA Before Close

- [ ] 4 Go-specific agent files created in .claude/agents/
- [ ] Zero Python-specific references in Go agent files
- [ ] All reference Go quality gate commands (go build, go test, golangci-lint, etc.)
- [ ] Translation protocol included in developer-go.md
- [ ] Anti-hallucination rules included in developer-go.md and tech-lead-go.md

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on alty-ok6.1 (scaffold paths must exist to reference in agent files)

title: Create Go migration team-prompt.md for Layer 1 (Domain)
description:
## Goal / Problem

Create the team prompt for Layer 1 (Domain) migration. This is the single source of truth that the tech-lead reads before launching the team. It must contain: mission, roster, file ownership, translation protocol, communication rules, quality gates, and settled design decisions.

## Background / Context

- Team launch template: .notes/example-team-launch.md
- Layer 1 design: docs/research/20260307_3_go_migration_team_execution.md (Q1 + Q4 sections)
- Bounded context mapping: docs/research/20260307_3_go_migration_team_execution.md (Layer 1 table)
- Anti-hallucination: docs/research/20260307_2_go_team_development_patterns.md

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | All domain contexts (14 bounded contexts) |
| Ubiquitous Language | bounded context, aggregate, value object, domain event, sentinel error |
| Layer | Domain (entire layer is the migration target) |

## Design

### Output File

.notes/go-migration-team-prompt-layer1.md (follows .notes/example-team-launch.md structure)

### Supporting File

.notes/go-migration-order-layer1.md (dependency graph between bounded contexts within Layer 1)

### Content Sections Required

1. Mission — translate Python domain layer to Go with test parity
2. Reference files list
3. Team roster (6 agents: 4 devs + TL + QA)
4. File ownership table mapping Python source → Go target per dev
5. Translation protocol (10 steps)
6. Anti-hallucination rules
7. Communication protocol (who reports to whom, structured format)
8. Quality gates (Go-specific)
9. Settled design decisions
10. Python source file → Go package mapping table

### File Ownership (Layer 1)

| Agent | Python Source Files | Go Target Packages |
|-------|--------------------|--------------------|
| dev-domain-core | domain/models/errors.py, domain/models/scenario.py, domain/models/stack_profile.py, domain/models/tech_stack.py, domain/models/domain_model.py, domain/models/domain_values.py | internal/shared/domain/errors/, internal/shared/domain/, internal/shared/domain/ddd/ |
| dev-discovery | domain/models/discovery_session.py, domain/models/bootstrap_session.py, domain/services/discovery_*.py | internal/discovery/domain/, internal/bootstrap/domain/ |
| dev-ticket | domain/models/ticket_values.py, domain/models/freshness.py, domain/services/ticket_*.py, domain/services/freshness_*.py | internal/ticket/domain/ |
| dev-features | domain/models/fitness_values.py, domain/models/quality_gate.py, domain/models/knowledge_entry.py, domain/models/rescue_*.py, domain/models/research.py | internal/fitness/domain/, internal/quality/domain/, internal/knowledge/domain/, internal/rescue/domain/, internal/research/domain/ |

## SOLID Mapping

N/A — documentation file, not code.

## TDD Workflow

N/A — documentation file. Validated by content review.

## Steps

1. Read .notes/example-team-launch.md for structural template
2. Read research report Q1 + Q4 for Layer 1 specifics
3. Create .notes/go-migration-order-layer1.md with bounded context dependency graph
4. Create .notes/go-migration-team-prompt-layer1.md following template structure
5. Fill team roster section with 6 agents (4 devs + TL + QA)
6. Fill file ownership table with exact Python→Go file mappings
7. Fill settled design decisions (from research reports)
8. Fill communication protocol (from research report Q3)
9. Review: verify every Python domain source file appears in exactly one agent's ownership
10. Review: verify no file appears in two agents' ownership (conflict check)

## Edge Cases

N/A — documentation file.

## Quality Gates

No code quality gates. Content validation:

- [ ] Every Python file under src/domain/ appears in exactly one agent's file ownership
- [ ] No Python file appears in two agents' ownership
- [ ] All 10 sections from Design are present
- [ ] Quality gate commands are Go-specific (no Python references)
- [ ] Communication protocol matches research report format

## Pre-Implementation Validation

- [ ] alty-ok6.1 scaffold exists (Go directory structure to reference)
- [ ] alty-ok6.2 agent templates exist (agent names to reference in roster)
- [ ] .notes/example-team-launch.md exists as structural reference
- [ ] Research report Layer 1 table is accurate (bounded context → agent mapping)

## QA Before Close

- [ ] Team prompt can be copy-pasted into a Claude Code session to launch the team
- [ ] File ownership has zero conflicts
- [ ] All Python domain files accounted for
- [ ] Settled design decisions list is complete

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on alty-ok6.1 (scaffold directory paths)
- Depends on alty-ok6.2 (agent template names)
- Risk: Python file inventory may have changed since research report — verify before writing

title: Shared kernel: errors, enums, base value objects, domain events
description:
## Goal

Translate shared kernel types from Python to Go: sentinel errors, base value objects (stack_profile, tech_stack, domain_values, quality_gate, persona, preview), and domain events (domain_events, config_events). These are imported by every other bounded context — must be completed first.

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Shared kernel (cross-cutting) |
| Layer | Domain |
| Go Package | `internal/shared/domain/errors/`, `internal/shared/domain/valueobjects/`, `internal/shared/domain/events/`, `internal/shared/domain/ddd/` |

## Owner

title: Watermill event bus: GoChannel + CQRS wiring (deferred to Epic 2)
description:
## Goal / Problem

Set up the Watermill event bus with GoChannel backend for local/CLI use. Wire CQRS-lite command routing and domain event publishing/subscribing. This enables handlers to publish domain events that other contexts can subscribe to.

## Background / Context

- **Research:** `docs/research/20260306_2_go_ddd_event_systems_messaging.md` — Watermill selected over alternatives (NATS, custom channels)
- **Research:** `docs/research/20260307_6_watermill_cqrs_deep_dive.md` — Full deep dive: Message structure, CQRS setup, GoChannel specifics, middleware, DDD integration, migration path to NATS
- **Research:** `docs/research/20260306_5_go_rewrite_evaluation.md` — 9 domain event types to route
- **Pattern reference:** Python domain events in `src/domain/events/` — already translated to Go in `internal/shared/domain/events/`

### Why Watermill

| Factor | Decision |
|--------|----------|
| GoChannel backend | Zero infrastructure for CLI tool (in-process pub/sub) |
| CQRS module | Built-in CommandBus + EventBus separation |
| Migration path | GoChannel → NATS when distributed mode needed |
| Middleware | Retry, recovery, correlation ID — production-ready |
| DDD fit | Message → domain event serialization via Marshaler |

### Domain Events (from Epic 1, already in Go)

| Event | Source Context | Consumer Context(s) |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| `DiscoveryCompleted` | Guided Discovery | Domain Model |
| `DomainModelGenerated` | Domain Model | Architecture Testing, Ticket Pipeline, Tool Translation |
| `FitnessTestsGenerated` | Architecture Testing | File Generation |
| `TicketPlanApproved` | Ticket Pipeline | Beads (Infrastructure) |
| `ConfigsGenerated` | Tool Translation | File Generation |
| `BootstrapCompleted` | Bootstrap | (terminal event) |
| `PersonaDetected` | Guided Discovery | (internal to Discovery) |
| `TicketFlagged` | Ticket Freshness | (notification) |
| `FlagCleared` | Ticket Freshness | (notification) |

### CQRS-lite Architecture (from 20260307_6)

```
              CommandBus (Watermill)
             /                      \
Command Handler 1              Command Handler 2
     |                              |
Domain Event ──→ EventBus (GoChannel) ──→ Event Handler
```

- **CommandBus**: Routes commands to exactly one handler
- **EventBus**: Routes domain events to zero or more subscribers
- **GoChannel**: In-process pub/sub (no external broker for CLI)
- **CQRS-lite**: Commands MAY return domain objects (not strict "error only")

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Shared (event bus is cross-cutting infrastructure) |
| Ubiquitous Language | Event, Command, Bus, Publisher, Subscriber, Handler |
| Layer | Infrastructure (event bus wiring) + Application (event routing) |

## SOLID Mapping

| Principle | Implementation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **S** | Event bus only handles message routing — no business logic |
| **O** | New event types added by implementing Marshaler, no bus changes |
| **L** | All event handlers honor the subscriber contract |
| **I** | Publisher and Subscriber are separate interfaces |
| **D** | Handlers depend on Publisher interface, not GoChannel directly |

## TDD Workflow

### RED Phase

Write tests for:
1. GoChannel pub/sub — publish event, subscriber receives it
2. Command routing — command dispatched to correct handler
3. Event fan-out — one event, multiple subscribers
4. Serialization — domain event → Message → domain event roundtrip

### GREEN Phase

1. Add Watermill dependency: `go get github.com/ThreeDotsLabs/watermill`
2. Create `internal/shared/infrastructure/eventbus/` package
3. Implement GoChannel publisher/subscriber setup
4. Implement CQRS CommandBus and EventBus
5. Create event Marshaler for domain events (JSON serialization)
6. Wire event subscriptions (DiscoveryCompleted → DomainModel handler, etc.)

### REFACTOR Phase

- Add middleware: correlation ID, recovery, logging
- Verify no goroutine leaks (proper context cancellation)
- Verify event ordering within a single topic

## Steps

1. Add Watermill to `go.mod`: `github.com/ThreeDotsLabs/watermill`
2. Create `internal/shared/infrastructure/eventbus/bus.go` — GoChannel setup
3. Create `internal/shared/infrastructure/eventbus/marshaler.go` — JSON marshaler for domain events
4. Create `internal/shared/infrastructure/eventbus/publisher.go` — Publisher wrapper
5. Create `internal/shared/infrastructure/eventbus/subscriber.go` — Subscriber wrapper
6. Create `internal/shared/infrastructure/eventbus/bus_test.go` — integration tests
7. Wire event routing: map each domain event to its topic and subscribers
8. Add middleware: recovery (panic → error), correlation ID propagation
9. Create `Publisher` interface in `internal/shared/application/` for DIP

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Watermill GoChannel pub/sub working — events published and received
- [ ] CQRS-lite CommandBus routes commands to correct handlers
- [ ] EventBus routes domain events to correct subscribers
- [ ] All 9 domain event types have JSON marshaling roundtrip tests
- [ ] Event fan-out works (DomainModelGenerated → 3 subscribers)
- [ ] Graceful shutdown — no goroutine leaks on context cancellation
- [ ] Middleware: recovery + correlation ID
- [ ] `go build ./...` and `go test -race` pass
- [ ] No event ordering violations within a single topic

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| No subscribers | Event published to empty topic | No error, event discarded |
| Subscriber panic | Handler panics | Recovery middleware catches, logs error |
| Context cancelled | Shutdown during event processing | Graceful stop, no goroutine leak |
| Malformed event | Invalid JSON payload | Unmarshal error, event nacked |

## Quality Gates

```bash
go build ./...
go test ./internal/shared/infrastructure/eventbus/... -v -race
go vet ./...
golangci-lint run
```

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on: 4xd.1 (ARCHITECTURE.md must document event bus architecture)
- Depends on: ok6.4 (domain events already translated to Go — CLOSED ✓)
- Risk: Watermill GoChannel is in-memory only — state lost on process restart (acceptable for CLI)
- Risk: CQRS module API may require specific message format — verify with 20260307_6 examples

1. Read Python source + test → Write Go test (RED) → Write Go impl (GREEN) → Refactor

3. Constructor pattern: `NewXxx() (*T, error)` for validated types
4. Sentinel errors with `errors.Is()` checking

## Strategy
Work context-by-context, smallest first:
1. `internal/challenge/domain/` (1 source, 1 test) — quick win
2. `internal/dochealth/domain/` (1 source, 2 tests)

4. `internal/knowledge/domain/` (2 sources, 2 tests)
5. `internal/rescue/domain/` (0 model sources, 1 event file) — events only
6. `internal/tooltranslation/domain/` (2 sources, 4 tests)
7. `internal/fitness/domain/` (4 sources, 5 tests) — largest, do last

## Translation Protocol
1. Read Python source + test → Write Go test (RED) → Write Go impl (GREEN) → Refactor
2. Table-driven tests with `t.Parallel()` and testify assert/require
3. Constructor pattern: `NewXxx() (*T, error)` for validated types
4. Sentinel errors with `errors.Is()` checking

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 16 Python source files translated to Go across 7 context packages
- [ ] All 19 Python test files translated with equivalent assertions
- [ ] `go build ./internal/fitness/... ./internal/knowledge/... ./internal/rescue/... ./internal/research/... ./internal/dochealth/... ./internal/challenge/... ./internal/tooltranslation/...` passes
- [ ] `go test` with `-v -race` passes for all 7 context packages
- [ ] `golangci-lint run` passes for all 7 context packages
- [ ] Domain services (canvas_assembler, challenger_service) included in their context packages
- [ ] QualityGate imported from `internal/shared/domain/valueobjects/` (not local)

title: Add serialization to DiscoverySession for crash recovery
description:
## Goal / Problem

Add optional to_snapshot() / from_snapshot() serialization to the DiscoverySession aggregate, enabling future persistence and crash recovery for MCP discovery sessions. This is the optional persistence path identified in the k7m.29 spike (Option 4 Hybrid).

## Background / Context

- **Spike output:** docs/research/20260301_mcp_multi_turn_sessions.md (Section 8, Ticket 2)
- **Decision:** Optional enhancement — not required for initial SessionStore implementation
- **Pattern reference:** DiscoverySession aggregate (src/domain/models/discovery_session.py)
- **SessionStore (alty-x9d):** In-memory store; this ticket adds serialization so SessionStore can optionally persist to filesystem or SQLite in the future

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Guided Discovery (Core) |
| Ubiquitous Language | DiscoverySession, snapshot, persona, playback, phase |
| Layer | Domain (serialization methods on aggregate) |

## Design

### Data Models

| Model | Type | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| DiscoverySession.to_snapshot() | Method | Serialize session state to dict |
| DiscoverySession.from_snapshot() | Classmethod | Restore session from serialized state |

### Snapshot Fields

All internal state must survive the round-trip:

- session_id, readme_content, status, persona, register, answers, skipped, playback_confirmations, _answers_since_last_playback

Note: `_answers_since_last_playback` (int counter at discovery_session.py:84) controls playback timing. Omitting it would silently reset the counter on restore, corrupting playback cadence.

### Sequence / Flow

```
SessionStore          DiscoverySession         Filesystem (future)
    |                       |                        |
    |-- to_snapshot() ----->|                        |
    |<-- dict --------------|                        |
    |-- json.dumps(dict) --------------------------------->|
    |                       |                        |
    |<-- json.loads(data) ---------------------------------|
    |-- from_snapshot(dict)->|                        |
    |<-- session ------------|                        |
```

## SOLID Mapping

| Principle | Implementation |
|-----------|----------------|
| **S**ingle Responsibility | Serialization is on the aggregate itself (knows its own state) |
| **O**pen/Closed | SessionStore can add persistence without modifying its interface |
| **L**iskov Substitution | from_snapshot produces a valid DiscoverySession honoring all invariants |
| **I**nterface Segregation | to_snapshot/from_snapshot are optional methods, not part of DiscoveryPort |
| **D**ependency Inversion | Serialization uses plain dicts — no framework dependency |

## TDD Workflow

### RED Phase

```python
# tests/domain/test_discovery_session_serialization.py
def test_snapshot_round_trip_empty_session():
def test_snapshot_round_trip_with_persona():
def test_snapshot_round_trip_with_answers():
def test_snapshot_round_trip_with_playback():
def test_snapshot_round_trip_completed_session():
def test_from_snapshot_invalid_data_raises():
def test_snapshot_contains_all_fields():
def test_snapshot_preserves_playback_counter():
```

### GREEN Phase

1. Add to_snapshot() method to DiscoverySession
2. Add from_snapshot() classmethod to DiscoverySession
3. Implement minimal serialization/deserialization

### REFACTOR Phase

- Ensure round-trip fidelity (session == from_snapshot(to_snapshot(session)))
- Verify no invariant violations on restore

## Steps

1. Add to_snapshot() to DiscoverySession — serializes session_id, readme_content, status, persona, register, answers, skipped, playback_confirmations, _answers_since_last_playback to a plain dict
2. Add from_snapshot() classmethod — reconstructs DiscoverySession from dict without re-running invariant checks (internal construction)
3. Write round-trip serialization tests for all reachable session states
4. (Follow-up) Updating SessionStore to support persistence callbacks is out of scope — track separately if needed

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] to_snapshot() returns a JSON-serializable dict with all session state (including _answers_since_last_playback)
- [ ] from_snapshot() reconstructs a valid DiscoverySession from that dict
- [ ] Round-trip: from_snapshot(to_snapshot(session)) produces equivalent session
- [ ] All 5 reachable session states can be serialized and restored (CREATED, PERSONA_DETECTED, ANSWERING, PLAYBACK_PENDING, COMPLETED)
- [ ] Playback counter (_answers_since_last_playback) survives round-trip
- [ ] Restored session continues to enforce all invariants
- [ ] No external dependencies added to domain layer
- [ ] Quality gates pass

Note: CANCELLED is defined in DiscoveryStatus but unreachable — no cancel() command exists on the aggregate. Serialization for CANCELLED is deferred until a cancel flow is implemented.

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Output |
|------|-------|-----------------|
| Empty session | CREATED state, no answers | Round-trip preserves state |
| Full session | COMPLETED with all 10 answers | Round-trip preserves all answers and playbacks |
| Mid-playback counter | ANSWERING with _answers_since_last_playback=2 | Counter preserved on restore |
| Missing field | Snapshot missing required key | ValueError or custom error |
| Corrupted data | Invalid status value | ValueError or custom error |
| Extra fields | Snapshot with unknown keys | Ignored gracefully |

## Quality Gates

```bash
uv run ruff check src/ tests/
uv run mypy .
uv run pytest tests/ -v --cov=src --cov-fail-under=80
```

- [ ] Lint passes
- [ ] Type check passes
- [ ] All tests pass with >= 80% coverage
- [ ] Edge cases have test coverage

## QA Before Close

- [ ] Round-trip works for all reachable session states
- [ ] Playback counter round-trip tested
- [ ] Edge cases covered
- [ ] No regressions in DiscoverySession behavior
- [ ] Domain layer still has zero external dependencies

## Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on: alty-x9d (SessionStore must exist first)
- Risk: Exposing internal state via to_snapshot breaks encapsulation — mitigate by making snapshot format opaque (dict, not public attributes)

title: Create /alty-brainstorm Claude Code skill for guided DDD discovery
description:
## Goal / Problem

Create `/alty-brainstorm` — a Claude Code skill that **ships as part of alty-cli** and gets installed into user projects during `alty init`. This is a core product feature: the conversational interface that turns a vague idea into structured DDD artifacts (PRD, DDD.md, ARCHITECTURE.md) through guided dialogue.

**Product context (PRD §4, §5):** alty's vision is "someone with an idea describes it in 4-5 sentences → alty guides them through structured DDD questions → progressively generates PRD, domain model, bounded contexts, architecture, and beads ticket structure." This skill delivers that vision for Claude Code users immediately, without waiting for the Python CLI engine (k7m.17).

**Why a Claude Code skill, not just Python CLI:**
- Claude Code is alty-cli's primary target environment (PRD §6: "CLI for humans, MCP for AI tools")
- A skill works today — no dependency on the Typer CLI chain (k7m.13 → k7m.14 → k7m.17)
- Users get the guided DDD experience the moment they run `alty init` and open Claude Code
- The skill serves as a living specification for the Python engine (k7m.17) — same questions, same artifact format, same quality
- Other AI tools (Cursor, OpenCode) will get equivalent skills via multi-tool config generation (PRD §5: C14)

**Inspiration:** The superpowers brainstorming skill delivered an excellent guided conversation for alty-platform's design. But it had no DDD awareness — it produced a PRD without domain stories, bounded contexts, ubiquitous language, or subdomain classification. This skill brings DDD structure to the same quality of conversational experience.

## Background / Context

### Research Foundation
- **10-question DDD framework:** `docs/research/20260222_guided_ddd_question_framework_consolidated.md` — 5 phases, dual register (technical/non-technical), playback loops, persona detection, question-to-artifact mapping
- **Subdomain classification:** `docs/research/20260222_subdomain_classification_complexity_budget.md` — Khononov's 3-step decision tree, treatment levels per classification
- **Persona-adapted discovery:** `docs/research/20260222_ddd_persona_adapted_discovery.md` — plain language question pairs, non-technical register

### Templates (output format)
- `docs/templates/PRD_TEMPLATE.md` — 8-section PRD structure
- `docs/templates/DDD_STORY_TEMPLATE.md` — Domain stories, ubiquitous language, bounded contexts, aggregates
- `docs/templates/ARCHITECTURE_TEMPLATE.md` — Layers, fitness functions, integration patterns

### Superpowers Brainstorming Patterns (to adopt)
- One question at a time (never overwhelm)
- Multiple-choice when possible (easier to answer)
- Playback in user's own words after each section
- Hard gate before implementation (no artifacts until discovery complete)
- Section-by-section approval for generated output
- YAGNI ruthlessly

### Existing alty Commands (conventions to follow)
- `.claude/commands/doc-health.md` — doc freshness checking
- `.claude/commands/architecture-docs.md` — architecture lookup
- `.claude/commands/prd-traceability.md` — PRD coverage verification

### Real Brainstorming Session Reference
- Session `4f4356b0` (vibe-seed project) — brainstormed alty-platform using superpowers. User inputs were short and directional ("yes Approach B", "like opensource?", "can we observe pencil.dev?"). The skill should support this lightweight interaction style.

### Relationship to Python CLI
- **k7m.17** (alty guide) — the Python CLI engine for the 10-question flow
- **k7m.18** (alty generate artifacts) — artifact generation from discovery answers
- This skill is the "zero-infra" version that works today via Claude Code
- Both should share the same question framework and artifact formats
- The skill is the specification; the CLI is the programmatic implementation

## DDD Alignment

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| Bounded Context | Guided Discovery (CORE subdomain) |
| Ubiquitous Language | DiscoverySession, Persona, Register, QuestionPhase, Playback, DomainStory, BoundedContext, SubdomainClassification, ComplexityBudget, UbiquitousLanguage |
| Layer | Product artifact (Claude Code skill shipped to users), not internal tooling |

## Design

### Where It Lives

```
alty-cli/
├── .claude/commands/
│   └── alty-brainstorm.md          # <- THIS SKILL (used during alty-cli development too)
├── src/
│   └── ...                         # Python CLI (k7m.17, future)
└── docs/templates/
    ├── PRD_TEMPLATE.md             # Output format for PRD
    ├── DDD_STORY_TEMPLATE.md       # Output format for DDD.md
    └── ARCHITECTURE_TEMPLATE.md    # Output format for ARCHITECTURE.md
```

During `alty init`, the skill gets copied to the user's project:
```
user-project/
├── .claude/commands/
│   └── alty-brainstorm.md          # Copied from alty-cli
```

### Skill Flow (7 Phases)

**Phase 0: Context Scan**
- Read existing project files (README, docs/, any existing DDD artifacts)
- Detect if this is a new project or extending an existing one
- If existing DDD.md found, ask: extend existing domain or start fresh?

**Phase 1: Persona Detection**
- Ask: "Before we start, which best describes you?"
  1. Developer or technical lead → TECHNICAL register
  2. Product owner or business person → NON_TECHNICAL register
  3. Domain expert (you know the problem area deeply) → NON_TECHNICAL register
  4. Not sure / mixed → NON_TECHNICAL register
- This determines which question variant is shown for all subsequent phases

**Phase 2: Seed Extraction**
- AI reads the README/description (user can also paste or link to a document)
- Extract candidate actors, work objects, and events
- Present: "Here's what I understand so far: [summary]. Does this capture the core idea?"

**Phase 3: Guided Questions (10Q Framework)**
10 questions in 5 sub-phases, using the correct register:

| Sub-Phase | Questions | Produces |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Actors & Work Objects | Q1 (who), Q2 (what things) | Actor list, entity candidates |
| Primary Story | Q3 (happy path), Q4 (failure), Q5 (other workflows) | Domain stories, commands, invariants |
| Events & Rules | Q6 (events), Q7 (policies), Q8 (read models) | Domain events, policies, query needs |
| Boundaries | Q9 (bounded contexts), Q10 (subdomain classification) | Context map, complexity budget |

Rules:
- One question at a time
- Multiple-choice when the domain allows (e.g., Q10 uses Khononov's decision tree as guided options)
- Open-ended when domain knowledge is needed (e.g., Q3 "walk me through the scenario")
- Playback after every 3-4 questions: "Let me play back what I heard. [Summary in user's words]. Did I get that right?"
- User can correct, amend, or go back during playback
- Quick mode: minimum 5 questions (Q1, Q3, Q4, Q9, Q10) for experienced users

**Phase 4: Approach Proposals**
- Based on discovered domain complexity, present 2-3 architecture approaches:
  - E.g., "Monolith with clear module boundaries" vs "Modular monolith with event bus" vs "Microservices"
  - Recommendation based on team size, complexity budget, subdomain count
- User selects approach

**Phase 5: Artifact Generation**
Generate three documents using templates:
1. **PRD** (`docs/PRD.md`) — from `docs/templates/PRD_TEMPLATE.md`
2. **DDD.md** (`docs/DDD.md`) — from `docs/templates/DDD_STORY_TEMPLATE.md`
3. **ARCHITECTURE.md** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`) — from `docs/templates/ARCHITECTURE_TEMPLATE.md`

Each artifact is presented **section by section** for approval. User can request changes to any section before moving on.

**Phase 6: Ticket Generation (Optional)**
- Ask if user wants beads tickets generated from DDD artifacts
- If yes, generate dependency-ordered epics using beads templates
- Preview ticket structure before creation
- Set formal dependencies via `bd dep add`

**Phase 7: Commit & Next Steps**
- Save all approved artifacts to `docs/`
- Suggest commit (user confirms)
- Recommend next steps: "Run `/doc-health` to verify, or start implementation with the generated tickets"

### Key Differences from Superpowers Brainstorming

| Aspect | Superpowers | /alty-brainstorm |
|--------|-------------|------------------|
| Domain awareness | Generic product design | DDD-structured (bounded contexts, aggregates, events) |
| Question framework | Ad-hoc clarifying questions | Structured 10Q in 5 phases from research |
| Output | Free-form design doc | Structured PRD + DDD.md + ARCHITECTURE.md |
| Register | Single | Dual (technical / non-technical) |
| Playback | Informal | Formal after every 3-4 questions |
| Classification | None | Khononov's Core/Supporting/Generic decision tree |
| Architecture | Approach recommendation only | Full doc with fitness functions, layer rules, complexity budget |
| Tickets | None (defers to writing-plans) | Optional beads ticket generation from DDD artifacts |
| Target user | Developer brainstorming features | 5 personas: developer, team lead, PO, domain expert, AI tool switcher |

## Steps

1. Study superpowers brainstorming skill file format and instruction structure
2. Study existing alty `.claude/commands/` for local conventions (frontmatter, style)
3. Write `.claude/commands/alty-brainstorm.md` incorporating all 7 phases
4. Include the full 10-question framework with both register variants inline
5. Reference templates for output format (point to `docs/templates/`)
6. Include Khononov's decision tree for subdomain classification (Phase 3, Q10)
7. Add quick-mode support (5 MVP questions for experienced users)
8. Add existing-project support (detect existing DDD.md, offer extend vs fresh)
9. Test the skill in a Claude Code session with a sample project idea
10. Iterate on question phrasing, playback quality, artifact quality

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `.claude/commands/alty-brainstorm.md` exists and is invocable via `/alty-brainstorm`
- [ ] Starts with persona detection (4 options matching research framework)
- [ ] Reads existing project context (README, docs/) before questions
- [ ] Asks questions one at a time using correct register for detected persona
- [ ] Technical register shown for developers; non-technical for PO/domain expert/mixed
- [ ] Playback after every 3-4 questions using user's own words
- [ ] User can correct/amend/go back during playback
- [ ] Hard gate: no artifact generation until discovery questions are validated
- [ ] Generates PRD matching `docs/templates/PRD_TEMPLATE.md` format
- [ ] Generates DDD.md matching `docs/templates/DDD_STORY_TEMPLATE.md` format
- [ ] Generates ARCHITECTURE.md matching `docs/templates/ARCHITECTURE_TEMPLATE.md` format
- [ ] Each artifact presented section-by-section for approval
- [ ] Subdomain classification uses Khononov's 3-step decision tree
- [ ] Complexity budget reflected in architecture (Core = full DDD, Supporting = simpler, Generic = buy)
- [ ] Ubiquitous language uses exact terms from user's answers (not imposed jargon)
- [ ] Quick mode available (5 MVP questions: Q1, Q3, Q4, Q9, Q10)
- [ ] Works for new projects AND extending existing DDD-structured projects
- [ ] Optional beads ticket generation with dependency ordering
- [ ] Skill file can be copied to user projects during `alty init` (self-contained, references only relative template paths)

## Edge Cases

| Case | Input | Expected Behavior |
|------|-------|-------------------|
| "Just build it" | Impatient user | Explain why discovery matters, offer quick mode (5Q) |
| Existing DDD.md | Project already has artifacts | Read existing, ask: extend or fresh? |
| Single bounded context | Simple project | Accept "just one context", skip context map details |
| User provides URL/doc | "Read this PRD first" | Use document as seed context (Phase 2) |
| Non-English domain | Domain uses non-English terms | Preserve original language in ubiquitous glossary |
| User changes mind | "Actually, rethink the actors" | Allow going back to any phase |
| Very large domain | 10+ BCs discovered | Focus on Core subdomains first, stub others |
| No README exists | Empty project | Ask user to describe idea in 4-5 sentences |
| PO with no tech knowledge | Persona = product_owner | Use only non-technical register, no DDD jargon in questions |
| User already knows DDD | Experienced developer | Technical register, can skip explanations, still follow framework |

## Quality Gates

- [ ] Skill file is valid Markdown with correct frontmatter for Claude Code command discovery
- [ ] Invocable via `/alty-brainstorm` in Claude Code
- [ ] Test run with sample project produces valid PRD, DDD.md, ARCHITECTURE.md matching template structures
- [ ] Generated artifacts pass `/doc-health` checks
- [ ] Non-technical register questions contain zero DDD jargon

## Risks / Dependencies

- **No blocking dependencies** — this is a Markdown skill file, independent of the Python CLI chain
- **Risk:** Quality depends on Claude following complex multi-phase instructions — may need iteration
- **Risk:** Different Claude models (Opus vs Sonnet vs Haiku) may produce different quality — test across models
- **Synergy:** Serves as living specification for k7m.17 (Python CLI engine) and k7m.18 (artifact generation)
- **Shipping:** Gets copied to user projects during `alty init` — must be self-contained and reference only relative paths
notes: AC addition (grooming 2026-02-23): Added fitness function AC item per PRD traceability gap C14 flagged in comment from 2026-02-23 07:02. New item: 'Fitness functions section of ARCHITECTURE.md is populated from discovered bounded context boundaries (not left as template placeholder)'. This requires Phase 5 to derive fitness function rules from subdomain classification + context map answers during ARCHITECTURE.md generation.
